text: The video shows the placement of surgical ports, ensuring they are positioned at least ten centimeters from the umbilicus to aid in mediastinal dissection. The surgeon inspects the peritoneal cavity and both hepatic lobes, confirming the absence of visible metastases.
The surgeon identifies an incarcerated umbilical hernia, which is visually reduced and repaired during the procedure.

The surgeon places a liver retractor and begins opening the thickened pars flaccida, which is visibly affected by prior radiation therapy. The dissection proceeds laterally towards the caudate lobe and continues towards the frontal esophageal ligament, with the thickened tissue posing a challenge due to neoadjuvant radiation therapy.

The surgeon creates a retroesophageal window and passes a Penrose drain to facilitate manipulation of the gastroesophageal junction and pulling down on the esophageal specimen during mediastinal dissection. Indocyanine Green (ICG) is used liberally for visualization.

The surgeon identifies the right gastroepiploic vessel and its branches, which serve as the feeding vessel for the gastric conduit. Care is taken to avoid injuring the vessel or grasping the fat above it to prevent vessel spasm and potential leakage. Firefly imaging is used intermittently to enhance visualization during the dissection.

The surgeon mixes 25 milligrams of ICG Green with 10 cc's of saline and administers 1.5 cc to enhance visualization of the vasculature. The ICG Green helps identify the blood supply to the future gastric conduit, ensuring the surgical approach remains far to the right on the screen.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the posterior wall of the stomach, avoiding the vascular pedicle to preserve the superior arcade and prevent vessel spasm. A visible fat pad is left intact to minimize manipulation of the blood supply to the gastric conduit. Edema is observed in the short posterior vessels, likely due to radiation therapy.

The surgeon performs cocorization of the duodenum, revealing the gastrocolic pedicle to the right and the developing groove between the duodenum and colon. Blunt dissection and a vessel sealer are used to identify tissue planes. ICG Green is applied to confirm perfusion of the gastric conduit, with vessels visibly illuminated superiorly.

The surgeon skeletonizes the left gastric pedicle down to its base, using a white load stapler to transect both the artery and vein simultaneously. This step is crucial for performing an adequate lymphadenectomy due to the presence of an avid lymph node around the gastric pedicle observed in preoperative imaging.

After transecting the gastric pedicle, the surgeon continues with transhiatal esophageal dissection, navigating through the radiated field towards the posterior mediastinum. The hiatus appears snug, prompting the surgeon to open it by transecting part of the right crus.

The surgeon performs ICG green perfusion to verify the integrity of the right gastroepiploic vessel, followed by the application of the final staple line using green and blue loads. The excellent perfusion is visible up to the tip of the gastric conduit. The Penrose drain is removed, and the specimen is pulled from the neck as the neck dissection is completed. The conduit is sutured to the specimen and carefully guided through the mediastinum to prevent twisting on the vascular pedicle, ensuring it is pulled without tension.
